FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study Release 5.1, N=495 EARS subsample at Year-4 follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis comparing passive (EARS) and self-reported smartphone/social media use with associations to cognitive, behavioral, and addiction outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in NPJ Mental Health Research, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use quantified via both self-report and EARS passive sensing on smartphones (94.26% of SM on phones per self-report). Paired with individual-trait-level outcomes: picture vocabulary (cognitive), externalizing symptoms (behavioral), internalizing symptoms (behavioral), SMAQ (addiction), VGAQ (addiction). SM is IV in regression models.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: β=0.06-0.07 for social media with externalizing symptoms (EARS and self-report); β=0.05 for internalizing; β=0.06-0.15 for SMAQ; β=0.05 for VGAQ; OR=0.96 for picture vocabulary and under-reporting

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses with reported β coefficients, ORs, and 95% CIs for SM↔outcome relationships

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (Year-4 snapshot with concurrent 3-week EARS period). EC7 does not apply to cross-sectional studies.
notes: Paper compares objective (EARS) and subjective (self-report) social media measures and examines differential associations with behavioral and addiction outcomes. Satisfies all inclusion criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=9465 children ages 9-11 at baseline, followed to Year 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis examining prospective association between baseline screen time and Year 2 binge-eating disorder with depression as mediator

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures generic composite 'average daily screen time' (TV, video games, social media, etc. bundled without isolation of phone/SM per modality). Methods state 'average daily screen time' without breakdown isolating smartphone or social media use. Per §2.4, multi-modality composites (TV+console+phone) with no per-device breakdown do not qualify. No separately measured SM/phone variable paired with outcome.

notes: Paper uses composite screen time without isolating phone or SM modality. Fails FT-IC2 due to lack of qualifying SM/phone variable. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=10,757 children ages 10-14 at baseline, prospective follow-up to Year 2 (2016-2020)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis examining associations between baseline parental monitoring and family conflict with screen time across six modalities at Years 1 and 2

FT_IC2.evidence: Six screen time modalities measured separately: TV shows/movies, YouTube videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media. Individual-trait-level predictors: parental monitoring (parenting behavior) and family conflict (family environment). Screen time variables (especially social media, YouTube, video games) are DV in analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: β=-0.37 for parental monitoring with total screen time; β=-0.16 for YouTube; β=-0.27 for video games; β=0.08 for family conflict with total screen time; β=0.03 for YouTube; β=0.02 for video games and TV

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential mixed-effects regression models with 95% CIs; standardized estimates reported for all screen modality outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline to Year 2 follow-up). Screen media variables (social media, YouTube, video games) are DV in all reported analyses. No analysis with smartphone/SM as IV exists anywhere in paper. All three EC7 conditions met. Exclusion code: FT-EC7.
notes: Paper meets IC1-IC4 but is excluded under EC7: longitudinal design with SM/phone exclusively as DV and no SM-as-IV analysis. Family/parenting factors (parental monitoring, family conflict) are predictors, not SM itself.
